chronology: 2024-01-15 [2024-01-15]: [visit] - Psychiatric evaluation conducted for patient John Doe who presented with symptoms of depression. Prescribed Sertraline 50mg. (Provider: Psychiatric evaluation) [Confidence: high]
2024-02-10 [2024-02-10]: [visit] - Follow-up visit. Patient reports improvement with medication and an increase in dose to 100mg. (Provider: Unknown) [Confidence: medium]

red_flags.issue: Provider identity not documented in follow-up visit. Medical record lacks identification of treating physician or healthcare provider responsible for medication dosage increase.
No documented diagnosis in follow-up visit despite ongoing psychiatric treatment and medication adjustment. Continuation of treatment without documented diagnostic assessment or confirmation.

Doubling of Sertraline dose (50mg to 100mg) within 26 days. Standard psychiatric practice typically recommends 4-6 weeks before dose adjustment to allow adequate time to assess therapeutic response and side effects.

No documentation of objective assessment measures, side effect monitoring, or clinical rationale for dose increase. Record only states patient 'reports improvement' without quantifiable metrics or clinical evaluation.

expert_opinions_needed.reason: Expert psychiatric opinion needed to evaluate whether increasing Sertraline from 50mg to 100mg after only 26 days meets standard of care, given typical recommendations for 4-6 week intervals before dose adjustments.
